questions.question: Write a short note on TRIGEMINAL NEURALGIA.
questions.answer:
A disorder of trigeminal nerve that causes episodes of sharp, stabbing pain
in the cheek, Lips, gums or chin on one side of the face.
People with this pain often wince or twitch, which is where trigeminal neuralgia
gets its French name "Tic douloureux" meaning painful twitch.

questions.question: Write a short note on Pain is a characteristic symptom.
questions.answer:
Occurs in bouts or paroxysms, sharp or lancinating in character
Radiates to the territary of one or more divisions of TN.
The pain is nearly always unilateral, and if bilateral, is successive
rather than concomitant
The mandibular & maxillary divisions are more commonly involved
than the opthatmic.
In some instances ,these 2 divisions may be simultaneously affected.
The pain may be exoked by a touch, a draft of air, any movement of the
face as in talking, chewing or yawning or swallowing.
The first episodes are usually fairly mild and brief, and it may be
few minutes or hours before the next attack.
With age the episodes become more frequent and painful, until the
patient begins to live in constant fear of next attack.
The momentary bursts of pain usually begins from the same spot on
the face each time. They are called trigger zones.
They are common on the vermillion barder of the lips, ala of the nase,
the cheeks and around the eyes.
The pt. learns to avoid touching the skin over the trigger zones which
frequently makes him go unwashed or unshaven for days.

questions.question: Write a short note on MODE OF ACTION OF LA.
questions.answer:
LA acts on voltage sensitive Na+ channels and block nerve conduction by
preventing /decreasing the entry of Na+ ions.
The Na+ channel has an activation gate near the extracellular mouth and
an inactivation gate at the intracellular mouth. In the resting state, the
activation gate is clased.
The LA receptor is located within the channel in its in atraceltular half.
The LA traverses the membrane in unionised form
reionises in the axoplasm and approaches the LA receptor
The receptor has higher affinity to the LA in its activated as
well as inactivedated state compared to resting state
Binding of LA to the receptor closes the Not channel &
prolongs the inactivated state
It prevents the entry of Na+ ions and thus action potential is not generated
conduction blocade k

questions.question: Write a short note on THERO THEORIES FOR MODE OF ACTION OF LA.
questions.answer:
1. ACETYLCHOLINE & THEORY - Acetylcholine is a neurotransmitter at the
nerve synapses is assumed to be involved in nerve conduction.
2. CALCIUM AISPLACEMENT THEORY - Depolarisation is prevented by
displacement of calcium from certain membrane sites that control the
permeability to sodium.
3. SURFACE CHARGE [REPULSION] THEORY - Local anaesthetic agents
binds to the nerve membrane and change the electrical potential at
the membrane surface.
buco LA molecules with net positive charge make the surface potential
at the nerve membrane more positive and reduce the excitability
of nerve by increasing threshold potential.
4. MEMBRANE EXPANSION THEORY - Local anaesthetic agents diffuse
to hydrophobic regions of excitable membrane and expand the
membrane, preventing the sodium permeability.
5. SPECIFIC RECEPTOR THEORY - LA agents act by binding to specific
receptors on sodium channels in the nerve membrane.
Once the receptor access is gained either on its external surface or
on the internal axoplasmic surface ,Nat permeability is decreased or
eliminated, interrupting the nerve conduction.

questions.question: Write a short note on BELL'S PALSY.
questions.answer:
Bell's palsy is defined as acute onset of non-suppurative inflammation
of facial nerve above the stylomastoid foramen, producing an unilateral
Lower Motor Neuron facial palsy.

questions.question: Describe BELL'S PALSY.
questions.answer:
It accounts for 60-70% of all cases of unilateral peripheral facial palsy.
Affects with equal frequency on the right and left sides of the face.
Either sex is affected equally.
May occur at any age, the median age is 40 years.
Increased incidence in the elderly diabetics hypertensives, pregnant
women than in the common people.

questions.question: What is the etiology of BELL'S PALSY?
questions.answer:
Cause is unclear, precipitating factors may be -
<a> viral infection
<b> vascular ischaemia
<c> autoimmune inflammatory disarders
<d> Heredity
<e> reactivation of HSV in geniculate ganglion
[Ramsey Hunt syndrome]

questions.question: Write a short note on CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS.
questions.answer:
Onset of Bells palsy is acute
ТИЗМЭРАИАМ
1/2 of the cases attain maximum paralysis in 48 hours
All cases are clinically prominent by 5 days.
Pain behind the ear may precede paralysis by 1-2 days.
Impairment of taste is present to some degree in all cases.
- but rarely beyond 2nd week of paralysis.
Hyperacusis or distartion of sound in ipsilateral ear
- paralysis of stapedius muscle
Patient feels stiffness of face pulled to one side.

Grade III - moderate dysfunction, eye can be completely closed with

Grade V - severe dysfunction, only barely perceptible motion,
asymmetry at rest'

questions.question: Describe the management of Grade I - Normal.
questions.answer:
A> SYMPTOMATIC
- Lubricating eye drops
- Protectionof the eyes during sleep - eye patches
- Massage of the weakened muscles
- Physiotherapy
B> MEDICOLNAL
- Prednisolone 60-80 mg/be day ^ in divided doses initial 4-5 days.
decreases possibility of permanent paralysis СИА
decreases severe pain
- Acyclovir 400 mg 5times/day
- Vitamins B1, B6, B12 may be administered
- If the patient is seen after 3-4 weeks, then steroid therapy is of
no use
— In hyperkinesia [ excessive restlessness]
- do Clostridium botulinum toxin
C> SURGICAL
- If electroneurography reveals a greater than 90% lass of compound
action potential within first 2 weeks following onset of paralysis, facial
nerve decompression is recommended. After this period, surgery is not
benefecial.
- Nerve decompression can be carried out internally or externally.
- Internal decompression - The nerve is exposed is exposed in the facial
canal pressure in the canal is relieved by exposing the nerve and the
epineural sheath is opened to visualise the nerve fibres and release adhesions
or re-establish continuity.
- External decompression - is done by releasing the epineural sheath
from surrounding scar tissue, bone or a foreign body.
- Nerve anastomosis - reanimation
- anastomasis of the central end of hypoglassal or spinal accessary
nerve with the distal end of the facial nerve is done
- Nerve grafting - whenever there is evidence of пеичота or loss of
portion of a nerve, nerve grafting can be considered.

questions.question: Write a short note on WORKING PRINCIPLE.
questions.answer:
Water boils when its vapour pressure equals to that of surrounding atmas-
- phere. Hence, when pressure inside a closed ЮЯТИОЭ vessel no increases the temp.
at which water boils also increases.
This principle has been applied in autoclave.
Bacteria are more susceptible to moist heat as bacterial protein
coagulates rapidly. Saturated steam can penetrate porous material
easily. When steam comes in contact with a cooler surface it condenses
to water and liberates it Patent heat to that surface.
The Large reduction in volume sucks in more steam to the same site and
the process continues till the temperature of the article is raised to that of
steam. The condensed water produces moist conditions for killing the
microbes present.

questions.question: Write a short note on HYPERBARIC OXYGEN THERAPY.
questions.answer:
Hyperbaric oxygen therapy for radiation damaged tissue was introduced
in 1973 by Greenwood, Gilchrist and Mainous.
Since there is a decrease in vascularity and cellularity of both hard and
soft tissues following irradiation of the bone, the hypoxic compromised
tissues when challenged by a traumatic episode fail to heal.
Delayed fibroblastic activity in neocollagen formation results in chronic
nonhealing wound. These wounds commonly get injected secondarily.
The goal of treating OML and asteoradionecrosis is to overcome hypoxia
& increase vascularity of tissues.
In order to increase tissue oxygenation, the patient is subjected to increa-
sed oxygen concentration and pressure.
HBO therapy consists of 100% oxygen administered at more than
1 absolute ospheric pressure. [ATA]
This 100% oxygen is a means of increasing the oxygen dissolved in plasma
and tissues that inturn induces angiogenesis in the compromised tissues.
formation

Each session is termed as dive. A total of 20-30 dives spanning 5 days
a week is planned preoperatively.

questions.question: What is the treatment for Nerve excitability tests?
questions.answer:
Open injuries of the facial nerve should be repaired surgically as soon as
possible.
Immediate primary repair at the time of intentional or unintentional
transection [neurotmesis] gives the best result.
Delayed primary repair or early secondary repair is acceptable, when delay T209
is necessary for certain reasons Like contaminated wound ,compromised medical
status, extensive tissue destruction etc.
Because of progressive distal nerve degeneration and muscle atrophy 12
months is often considered as the Limit for successful facial nerve
repair.

exercises are helpful to maintain muscle tone and prevent muscle
atrophy.

questions.question: How do you manage dental extractions in patients with bleeding disorders?
questions.answer: **PRE-OPERATIVE ASSESSMENT**: **1. History**: **Type of bleeding disorder**, **Severity**, **Previous bleeding episodes** (dental/surgical), **Medications** (anticoagulants, antiplatelets). **2. Laboratory tests**: **Hemophilia**: **Factor VIII** (Hemophilia A) or **Factor IX** (Hemophilia B) levels. **Anticoagulants**: **INR** (warfarin), **aPTT** (heparin). **General**: **Platelet count**, **PT**, **Bleeding time**. **3. Physician consultation**: Coordinate with hematologist/physician. **HEMOPHILIA**: **Factor replacement** essential. **1. Mild Hemophilia A** (Factor VIII 5-25%): **Desmopressin (DDAVP)** - releases stored Factor VIII (2-3x increase). **Dose**: 0.3 mcg/kg IV **1 hr before** procedure. **Alternative**: **Tranexamic acid** (antifibrinolytic) alone. **2. Moderate/Severe Hemophilia** (Factor VIII <5%): **Factor VIII concentrate** - raise level to **50-100%** before extraction (target depends on extent). **Dose**: **Loading**: 25-50 IU/kg pre-op, **Maintenance**: Continue post-op (7-10 days). **Hemophilia B**: **Factor IX concentrate** (similar protocol). **3. Local measures** (crucial): (a) **Atraumatic extraction** - minimize trauma, (b) **Sutures** - **primary closure** (figure-of-eight over socket), (c) **Hemostatic agents**: **Oxidized cellulose (Surgical)**, **Gelfoam**, **Fibrin glue** - pack socket, (d) **Tranexamic acid** mouthwash - **5% TXA** rinse QID x 7 days (prevents clot lysis), (e) **Avoid** aspirin, NSAIDs (use paracetamol). **4. Hospital setting** - NOT outpatient (need monitoring, factor availability). **WARFARIN (Anticoagulation)**: **1. Check INR**: **Therapeutic range** (2-3) - **safe** for simple extractions with local measures. **INR 2.5-3.5** - acceptable for minor oral surgery. **INR >4** - **defer**, adjust warfarin (consult physician). **2. DO NOT STOP warfarin** (risk of thromboembolism - stroke, MI). **Exception**: Major surgery (consult physician - may bridge with heparin). **3. Local hemostatic measures**: (a) **Minimize...

questions.question: Describe the phases of wound healing.
questions.answer: **Wound healing** - complex process restoring tissue integrity. **TYPES**: **Primary intention** - wound edges approximated (sutured) - minimal scarring. **Secondary intention** - wound left open (granulation fills defect) - more scarring. **Tertiary intention** (delayed primary) - initially open, closed later. **PHASES (Overlapping)**: **1. HEMOSTASIS (Immediate - minutes)**: **Vasoconstriction** → **Platelet aggregation** → **Clot formation** (fibrin mesh). **Prevents blood loss**, **scaffolding** for cell migration. **2. INFLAMMATORY PHASE (0-3 days)**: **a) Early (0-24 hrs)**: **Vasodilation** → **Increased permeability** → **Edema**, **erythema**, **pain**. **Neutrophils** arrive (phagocytose bacteria, debris) - peak 24 hrs. **b) Late (24-72 hrs)**: **Macrophages** arrive (phagocytose debris, **secrete growth factors** - crucial for healing progression). **Lymphocytes** (immune response). **Clinical**: **Swelling, redness, warmth, pain, loss of function** (cardinal signs of inflammation). **Prolonged inflammation** → **chronic wound**, **delayed healing**. **3. PROLIFERATIVE PHASE (3 days - 3 weeks)**: **Tissue formation**. **a) Granulation tissue formation** (3-5 days): **Fibroblasts** proliferate → **collagen synthesis** (Type III initially). **Angiogenesis** → **new capillaries** (pink, granular appearance). **Myofibroblasts** → **wound contraction**. **b) Epithelialization** (24-48 hrs onwards): **Keratinocytes** migrate across wound (from edges) → **cover defect**. **Faster** in **primary intention** (sutured - edges close). **c) Contraction**: **Myofibroblasts** pull wound edges together (reduces defect size - important in **secondary intention**). **4. REMODELING/MATURATION PHASE (3 weeks - 1 year)**: **Collagen remodeling**: **Type III collagen** replaced by **Type I** (stronger). **Collagen** reorganized along **tension lines**. **Scar maturation**: Initially **red, raised** → gradually **pale, flat** (12-18 months). **Tensile strength** increases (pla...

questions.question: Describe principles of suturing and commonly used suture materials.
questions.answer: **PRINCIPLES OF SUTURING**: **1. Aseptic technique** - sterile instruments, gloves. **2. Tissue handling**: **Gentle** - avoid crushing (tissue forceps with teeth, not hemostats). **3. Hemostasis**: **Control bleeding** before closure (pressure, cautery). **4. Dead space elimination**: **Close in layers** (prevent hematoma/seroma). **5. Eversion**: Wound edges **slightly everted** (not inverted) - better healing, less scar depression. **6. Tension-free**: **No tension** on wound edges (undermining if needed). **7. Accurate approximation**: **Align tissue layers** properly. **8. Knot security**: **Square knots** (surgeon's knot if tension). **SUTURE MATERIALS**: **ABSORBABLE** (degraded by body - no removal needed): **1. Catgut** (Plain/Chromic): **Natural** (collagen from sheep/cattle intestine). **Rapid absorption** (plain 7-10 days, chromic 14-21 days). **High tissue reaction**. **Rarely used** now. **2. Polyglycolic acid (Vicryl)**: **Synthetic**. **Absorption**: 60-90 days. **Moderate tissue reaction**. **Uses**: **Deep layers**, muscle, subcutaneous (where removal difficult). **3. Polyglactin (Vicryl Rapide)**: **Faster absorption** (10-14 days). **Uses**: **Oral mucosa** (intraoral - no removal needed). **NON-ABSORBABLE** (must be removed): **1. Silk**: **Natural** (braided). **Easy handling**, good knot security. **High tissue reaction** (inflammation). **Uses**: **Intraoral/skin** (extraoral) - **MOST COMMON** in oral surgery. **Removal**: **7 days**. **2. Nylon (Ethilon)**: **Synthetic** (monofilament). **Low tissue reaction**. **Less memory** (harder to tie). **Uses**: Skin closure (face). **3. Polypropylene (Prolene)**: **Synthetic** (monofilament). **Very low reaction**. **High memory** (springs back). **Uses**: Skin, cardiovascular. **4. Stainless steel**: **Very strong**, **inert**. **Difficult handling**. **Uses**: Bone wiring, tendon repair. **NEEDLE TYPES**: **Cutting** (triangular - cuts through tissue) - **SKIN, oral mucosa** (tough tissue). **Rev...

questions.question: Classify nerve injuries. How do you assess and manage paresthesia?
questions.answer: **NERVE INJURY CLASSIFICATION (Seddon)**: **1. NEUROPRAXIA**: **Mildest**. **Conduction block** (axon intact, myelin damaged). **No Wallerian degeneration**. **Recovery**: **Complete**, **spontaneous** (days-weeks). **Prognosis**: Excellent. **2. AXONOTMESIS**: **Moderate**. **Axon disrupted**, **endoneurium intact** (nerve sheath preserved). **Wallerian degeneration** occurs distal to injury. **Regeneration**: **Yes** (axons regrow along intact sheath - **1mm/day** / **1 inch/month**). **Recovery**: **Partial to complete** (weeks-months). **Prognosis**: Good (80-90% recovery). **3. NEUROTMESIS**: **Severe**. **Complete transection** (axon + endoneurium + perineurium). **Wallerian degeneration**. **Regeneration**: **Poor** (axons cannot find pathway - **neuroma** formation, misdirection). **Recovery**: **Minimal** without surgical repair. **Prognosis**: Poor unless **microsurgical repair**. **DENTAL SURGERY NERVE INJURIES**: **Inferior alveolar nerve (IAN)**: 3rd molar extraction, implants, mandibular fractures. **Lingual nerve**: 3rd molar extraction (lingual flap elevation), lingual anesthesia. **Incidence**: **Temporary** 0.5-5%, **Permanent** 0.1-1%. **ASSESSMENT OF PARESTHESIA**: **1. History**: **Onset** (immediate post-op vs delayed), **Duration**, **Progression** (improving/static/worsening), **Nature** (numbness, tingling, dysesthesia - abnormal sensation). **2. Clinical tests**: **a) Two-point discrimination**: **Normal**: 2-4mm (lower lip/tongue). **Impaired**: >6mm. **b) Light touch**: Cotton wisp/brush - **detect sensation**. **c) Pin-prick**: **Sharp/dull** discrimination. **d) Thermal**: Hot/cold discrimination. **3. Map extent**: Mark borders of anesthetic/paresthetic area (compare to normal side). **4. Baseline documentation**: **Immediate post-op** assessment (medico-legal). **5. Serial assessment**: **1 week, 1 month, 3 months, 6 months** (track recovery). **MANAGEMENT**: **IMMEDIATE (If injury suspected intra-op)**: **1. Inform patient** (documen...

questions.question: What are the indications for tracheostomy? Describe the procedure.
questions.answer: **Tracheostomy** - surgical creation of opening into **trachea** (through anterior neck) for airway access. **Temporary** (tube removable) or **Permanent**. **INDICATIONS**: **1. Upper airway obstruction**: (a) **Maxillofacial trauma** - bilateral mandibular fractures, midface fractures (tongue fall-back, bleeding), (b) **Ludwig's angina** - floor of mouth swelling → airway compromise, (c) **Tumors** - oral/pharyngeal/laryngeal (obstructing airway), (d) **Burns** - inhalational injury, facial burns, (e) **Angioedema** - severe allergic reaction. **2. Prolonged intubation**: **>7-10 days** mechanical ventilation (ICU patients) - **prevents laryngeal damage** from endotracheal tube. **3. Inability to intubate**: **Difficult airway** (limited mouth opening, anatomical anomalies). **4. Pulmonary toilet**: Facilitate **suctioning** of secretions (neuromuscular disorders, coma). **5. Prophylactic**: Before **major head-neck surgery** (airway edema expected post-op). **TYPES**: **Emergency** (cricothyrotomy - faster) vs **Elective** (tracheostomy - preferred if time permits). **PROCEDURE (Elective tracheostomy)**: **1. Position**: **Supine**, **neck extended** (shoulder roll), **head midline**. **2. Anesthesia**: **General** (preferred) or **Local** with sedation (awake tracheostomy). **3. Landmarks**: **Thyroid cartilage** (laryngeal prominence), **Cricoid cartilage** (below thyroid), **Tracheal rings** (below cricoid), **Sternal notch**. **4. Incision**: **Transverse** (2-3 cm) at level of **2nd-3rd tracheal ring** (midway between cricoid and sternal notch). **Alternative**: **Vertical** midline incision (faster - emergency). **5. Dissection**: (a) Divide **subcutaneous tissue**, **platysma** (muscle), (b) **Retract** strap muscles (sternohyoid, sternothyroid) **laterally**, (c) **Divide thyroid isthmus** (suture-ligate or retract superiorly) - exposes trachea. **6. Tracheal incision**: (a) **Identify tracheal rings** (palpate), (b) **Vertical incision** through **2nd-3r...

questions.question: Describe the extracranial course and branches of the facial nerve.
questions.answer: **Facial nerve (CN VII)** - **motor nerve** to muscles of **facial expression** + parasympathetic to salivary/lacrimal glands + taste (anterior 2/3 tongue via chorda tympani). **EXTRACRANIAL COURSE**: **1. Exit from skull**: Emerges from **stylomastoid foramen** (between styloid and mastoid processes). **2. Enters parotid gland**: Immediately enters **parotid gland** (runs through gland - **NOT supplies parotid** - supplied by glossopharyngeal CN IX). **3. Division**: Divides into **two main trunks** within parotid: **Temporofacial** (upper) and **Cervicofacial** (lower). **4. Further branching**: Forms **5 main terminal branches** (pes anserinus - "goose foot" pattern). **TERMINAL BRANCHES (memory: "**T**o **Z**anzibar **B**y **M**otor **C**ar")**: **1. TEMPORAL (Frontal)**: **Course**: Crosses **zygomatic arch** (superficial - vulnerable). **Supplies**: **Frontalis** (raises eyebrows), **orbicularis oculi** (upper part - eye closure), corrugator. **Injury**: **Inability to raise eyebrow**, wrinkle forehead. **At risk**: Trauma to temporal region, **parotidectomy**, **facelift surgery**. **2. ZYGOMATIC**: **Course**: Crosses **zygoma**. **Supplies**: **Orbicularis oculi** (lower part - eye closure). **Injury**: **Incomplete eye closure**, lagophthalmos (corneal exposure risk). **3. BUCCAL**: **Course**: Crosses **masseter muscle** (over buccal region). **Multiple branches** (interconnecting). **Supplies**: **Buccinator**, orbicularis oris (upper lip), muscles of nose. **Injury**: **Loss of smile** (upper lip), inability to puff cheeks (blow air). **At risk**: **Parotidectomy**, facial trauma. **LEAST serious** branch injury (multiple interconnections - compensation). **4. MARGINAL MANDIBULAR**: **Course**: Runs along **inferior border of mandible** (may dip **below** mandible up to 1 cm in 20% cases - danger zone). Crosses **facial vessels**. **Supplies**: **Lower lip** depressors (depressor labii inferioris, depressor anguli oris), mentalis. **Injury**: **Most CLI...

questions.question: What are the essential elements of informed consent for oral surgery?
questions.answer: **Informed consent** - **legal and ethical** requirement before any surgical procedure. Patient must **understand** and **voluntarily agree** to treatment after receiving adequate information. **ESSENTIAL ELEMENTS**: **1. COMPETENCE**: Patient must be **legally competent** (adult >18 years, sound mind). **Exceptions**: **Minors** (<18 years) - **parental consent** required. **Emergency** - implied consent (save life). **Incompetent patient** - guardian/next-of-kin consent. **2. DISCLOSURE OF INFORMATION**: **a) Diagnosis**: Patient's current condition (e.g., impacted tooth, abscess). **b) Proposed treatment**: Procedure planned (e.g., surgical extraction, I&D), **Alternative treatments** (e.g., observation, antibiotics), **Option of no treatment** (consequences). **c) Risks and complications**: **Common**: Pain, swelling, bleeding, infection, dry socket, trismus. **Serious** (even if rare): **Nerve injury** (IAN/lingual - numbness), **jaw fracture**, **sinus perforation**, **damage to adjacent teeth**, **TMJ problems**. **d) Benefits**: Expected outcome (pain relief, infection control, prevent complications). **e) Prognosis**: Expected recovery time, success rate. **3. UNDERSTANDING**: Patient must **comprehend** information (language appropriate, layman's terms - avoid medical jargon). **Verify understanding**: Ask patient to **repeat** key points. **Written + verbal** explanation (consent form + discussion). **4. VOLUNTARINESS**: Decision made **freely** without coercion, pressure, or undue influence. **5. AUTHORIZATION**: Patient **signs consent form** (written documentation). **DOCUMENTATION**: **1. Consent form**: **Signed**, **dated**, **witnessed**. **Contains**: Procedure name, risks disclosed, patient signature, doctor signature, witness. **2. Medical records**: **Note in records** - "Informed consent obtained, risks explained (nerve injury, bleeding, infection, dry socket, jaw fracture, sinus perforation), patient understood, all questions answered". **Spe...

questions.question: Define: Decortication
questions.answer: Surgical**: (a) **Remove source** - extract infected teeth, (b) **Incision & drainage** - if abscess, (c) **Sequestrectomy** - remove dead bone (sequestra), (d) **Saucerization** - create bony cavity for drainage/granulation, (e) **Decortication** - remove sclerotic bone (chronic), (f) **Resection** - if severe/refractory (rare)

questions.question: Write a short note on SUPPORTIVE MEASURES.
questions.answer:
1. Antibiotics - For prevention of secondary infection
- Selection of AB should be done on the basis of culture
and sensitivity testing
- Penicillin and its derivatives are the drug of choice.
- Penicillin V 250-500mg 6 hourly is adequate
[initially IV later switched to oral route]
- In case of penicillin resistance, a broad spectrum AB
2. Nasal decongestants - encourage drainage of pus and secretions
- Ephedrine nasal drops (0.5%), instilled intranasally
every 2-3 hours
The correct method of using this is the recumbent position
with the head inclined to the affected side.
- Benzoin and menthol inhalations
3. Analgesics - Aspirin 500 mg QDS
- Paracetamol 500 mg TDS
- Ibuprofen 400 mg TDS

questions.question: What is osteomyelitis of the jaw? Describe types and management.
questions.answer: **Osteomyelitis** is **infection of bone and bone marrow**. **Mandible** more common than maxilla (less vascularity, denser cortical bone). **TYPES**: **1. ACUTE OSTEOMYELITIS**: **Sudden onset**, **severe pain**, **fever**, **swelling**. **Cause**: Odontogenic infection spreads to bone (periapical abscess, fracture, surgery). **Features**: **Pus formation**, **sequestrum** formation (dead bone), **Involucrum** (new bone shell around dead bone). **2. CHRONIC OSTEOMYELITIS**: **Persistent** (>1 month), **low-grade**, **intermittent** symptoms. **Primary chronic** (direct infection - rare) or **Secondary** (from untreated acute). **Features**: **Draining sinus** (intraoral/extraoral), **Pathological fracture** (weak bone), **Sequestra** in CT/radiograph. **3. CHRONIC SCLEROSING OSTEOMYELITIS** (Garré's): **Non-suppurative** (no pus), **young patients**, mandibular **body/angle**. **Periosteal bone formation** → **cortical thickening** → "**onion-peel**" appearance (radiograph). **CLINICAL FEATURES**: **Acute**: Deep boring pain, fever, malaise, swelling, **paresthesia** (IAN involvement), **teeth mobile**, **pus discharge**, **trismus**. **Chronic**: Intermittent pain/swelling, **draining sinus**, **foul odor**, **sequestra** may be palpable/visible. **RADIOGRAPHIC**: **Early** - no changes (10-14 days). **Late**: **Irregular radiolucency**, **moth-eaten** appearance, **sequestra** (radiopaque), **involucrum**. **DIAGNOSIS**: **Clinical + Radiographic + Culture**. **Biopsy** if chronic (rule out malignancy). **MANAGEMENT**: **1. Antibiotics** (prolonged - **6-12 weeks**): **IV initially** (Clindamycin 600-900mg q8h or Ampicillin-Sulbactam), then **oral** (Clindamycin 300mg QID, Amoxicillin-Clavulanate). **Adjust** per culture/sensitivity. **2. Surgical**: (a) **Remove source** - extract infected teeth, (b) **Incision & drainage** - if abscess, (c) **Sequestrectomy** - remove dead bone (sequestra), (d) **Saucerization** - create bony cavity for drainage/granulation, (e...

cyst, evacuating the contents of the cyst, and maintaining continuity
between the cyst and the oral cavity, maxillary sinus or nasal cavity.
The only partion that is removed is the piece removed to produce the
window. The remaining cystic lining is left in situ.
It is also called decompression or Partisch I technique or cystotomy.
This process decreases intracystic pressure and promotes shrinkage of
the cyst and bone fill.

questions.question: What is the etiology of TRIGEMINAL NEURALGIA?
questions.answer:
Usually caused by contact between a normal blood vessel and the trigeminal
nerve at the base of the brain
This contact puts pressure on the nerve and causes it to malfunction
Can occur as a result of ageing, or it can be related to multiple sclerasis
or a similar disarder that damages the myelin sheath protecting
certain nerves.
Less commonly, TN can be caused by a tumor compressing the nerve.
Anomalies of superior cerebellar artery - most recently blamed cause

questions.question: What is the etiology of OSTIOMYELITIS?
questions.answer:
Odontogenic infections originating from pulpal or periodontal tissues,
pericaronitis, infected socket, infected cyst
Trauma - it is the second leading cause especially compound fracture
and surgery
Infections of orofacial region derived from
- -periastitis following gingival ulceration
- lymph nodes infected from furuncles
- lacerations
- peritonsillar abscess

questions.question: What are the radiographic features of Seldom associated with primary teeth?
questions.answer:
The cyst exhibits' a round, pear or ovoid shaped radiolucency, with
a thin, radiopaque line around the periphery of radiolucent area.
In case of very Large cysts' or infected cysts, the peripheral white line

questions.question: What is the treatment for Seldom associated with primary teeth?
questions.answer:
Non vital teeth that are associated with the cyst' are either extracted
or retained, by endodontic treatment and apicoectomy.
External sinus tracts should always be excised to prevent epithelial
ingrowth.
The commonly employed surgical procedure for radicular cysts' is
enudeation, with primary clasure.
Very small cysts can be removed through tooth socket
Large periodontal cysts that encroach upon the maxillary antrum
or inferior alveolar neurovascular bundle or the nase may be
treated by marsupialisation.

questions.question: Write a short note on DENTIGEROUS CYST.
questions.answer:
Dentigerous cyst is a developmental odontogenic cyst that is formed by the
expansion of dental follicle and therefore always attached to the neck of
the impacted or unerupted teeth.
It is also called as follicular cyst.
Paget (1863) coined the term dentigerous cyst.

questions.question: Describe the pathogenesis of DENTIGEROUS CYST.
questions.answer:
A> INTRAFOLLICULAR THEORY
It is caused by fluid accumulation between the REE and the examel
surface, resulting is a cyst in which the crown is located within the lumen.
B> EXTRAFOL.L.ICULAR THEORY
It may also arise by proliferation and custic transformation of islands of
odontogenic epithelium in connective tissue wall of dental follicle or even
outside dental follicle and this transformed epithelium then unite with
lining follicular epithelium farming cystic cavity around tooth crown.

questions.question: What are the radiographic features of DENTIGEROUS CYST?
questions.answer:
Well defined radiolucency around the impacted or unerupted teeth.
Usually unilocular but at times it may be multilocular.
Cysts have a well-defined sclerotic margin, unless when they are infected,
then the margins are poorly defined.

questions.question: What is the treatment for Three radiological variations are observed?

questions.question: Write a short note on MUCOCELE.
questions.answer:
Mucocele is the term used for swelling due to accumulation of saliva, as a
result of obstruction or trauma to the salivary gland ducts.
It is not a true cyst as it lacks an epithelial Lining.
There are 2 types of mucoceles -
A> extravasation type
B> retention type
These two types differ in the pathogenesis. The extravasation type is the
common one.

questions.question: Describe the pathogenesis of MUCOCELE.
questions.answer:
A> EXTRAVASATION TYPE
It is caused by trauma leading to laceration of the duct, commonly of the
minor salivary glands resulting in extravasation of the mucin into the
connective tissue.
The trauma may be caused by biting of the Lip, cheek, pinching of Lip by
extraction forceps etc.
The mucin, which extravasates into the connective tissue, leads to inflamm-
- ation, drawing the neutrophils and macrophages to the site, which in will turn
will result in the formation of the granulation tissue, which actually
forms the wall of these extravasation cysts.
B> RETENTION TYPE
This occurs due to obstruction of the salivary duct, commonly because of
a siabolith or may be due to periductal scar, ,or impinging tumor, resulting
in the accumulation of the saliva in the duct.
This leads to dilatation of the duct resulting in a cyst like lesion, These
cysts may show the presence of ductal epithelium histologically.

questions.question: Describe the clinical features of MUCOCELE.
questions.answer:
Mucoceles occur as painless swellings, which may be recurrent.
The common sites of occurrence are -
A> extravasation type - Lower Lip and tongue
B> retention type - palate, cheek, floor of mouth and maxillary sinus
A well-demarcated lesion of the upper lip could be a salivary gland
neoplasm than a mucocele.
The extravasation type occurs in the 2nd decade and also in children.
The retention type occurs in the middle-aged persons.
Based on the Location clinically, there are 2 types of mucoceles
a> Superficial
b> Deep type
The superficial mucocele resembles a vescicle or bulla.
Since they are placed superficially, the bluish translucency is seen.
They are fluctuant. Usually, these cysts' are 4mm to 1cm in size.
The wall is very thin and they rupture easily.
The deep mucoceles are covered by normal appearing mucasa thus
they are different from the superficial ones in the color.
They are firm, well- circumscribed lesions and are present for a
longer period.

- Marsupialisation - for simple ranula
it avoids surgical dissection and chancesof injury

questions.question: What is odontogenic keratocyst (KCOT)? Why is it clinically significant?
questions.answer: **Odontogenic keratocyst (OKC)** / **Keratocystic odontogenic tumor (KCOT)** - **aggressive developmental** odontogenic cyst. **Reclassified** as **tumor** by WHO (2005) due to aggressive behavior (changed back to cyst 2017). **CLINICAL SIGNIFICANCE**: **1. HIGH RECURRENCE** (25-60%) - **most aggressive** odontogenic cyst. **2. Local aggressiveness** - invades marrow spaces, difficult complete removal. **3. Association with syndromes** - **Gorlin-Goltz syndrome** (Nevoid basal cell carcinoma syndrome) - multiple OKCs + basal cell carcinomas + skeletal abnormalities. **CLINICAL FEATURES**: **1. Age**: **2nd-3rd decade** (young adults). **2. Location**: **Posterior mandible** (angle/ramus - 70%), maxilla (30%). **3. Often asymptomatic** - discovered on radiograph. **4. Slow expansion** - less swelling than dentigerous cyst (grows along bone - **anteroposterior** rather than lateral expansion). **5. Multiple cysts** - in Gorlin-Goltz syndrome. **RADIOGRAPHIC**: **1. Well-defined radiolucency** - **unilocular** (young) or **multilocular** (older/larger - "soap-bubble" appearance). **2. Scalloped margins** - irregular borders (grows into bone marrow spaces). **3. May surround unerupted tooth** - mimics dentigerous cyst. **4. **Root resorption** - adjacent teeth. **HISTOPATHOLOGY** (KEY diagnostic): **1. PARAKERATINIZED** stratified squamous epithelium (5-8 cell layers thick). **2. Corrugated/wavy surface** ("corrugated roofing iron"). **3. Palisaded basal cells** (columnar cells in picket-fence arrangement). **4. Thin fibrous wall**. **5. Satellite/daughter cysts** - in wall (explains high recurrence). **DIFFERENTIAL**: **Dentigerous cyst** (similar radiograph), **Ameloblastoma** (more aggressive, multilocular). **MANAGEMENT**: **CHALLENGE**: Balance **complete removal** (reduce recurrence) vs **preserve bone/teeth**. **OPTIONS**: **1. Enucleation alone**: **Simplest** but **highest recurrence** (60%). **2. Enucleation + Carnoy's solution**: Chemical cauterization of cys...

questions.question: What are the types of biopsy? When is each indicated?
questions.answer: **Biopsy** - removal of **tissue** for **histopathological examination** to establish definitive diagnosis. **TYPES**: **1. EXCISIONAL BIOPSY**: **Complete removal** of entire lesion with margin of normal tissue. **Indications**: (a) **Small lesions** (<1-1.5 cm), (b) **Pedunculated** lesions (stalked), (c) **Lesions with low malignancy suspicion** (clinical diagnosis benign), (d) **Therapeutic** - removes lesion completely (diagnosis + treatment). **Advantage**: **Single procedure** (diagnostic + curative). **Disadvantage**: If malignant, margins may be inadequate. **Examples**: Fibroma, mucocele, small benign tumors. **2. INCISIONAL BIOPSY**: **Removal of representative part** of lesion (wedge/punch). **Remaining lesion** left in situ. **Indications**: (a) **Large lesions** (>1.5 cm), (b) **High malignancy suspicion** - plan definitive surgery after confirmation, (c) **Multiple clinical appearances** - sample different areas, (d) **Deep-seated lesions**, (e) **Uncertain diagnosis**. **Technique**: **Wedge biopsy** - elliptical incision (includes normal + abnormal tissue), or **Punch biopsy** (4-6mm punch). **Advantage**: Less invasive, **plan definitive treatment** after diagnosis. **Examples**: Large oral ulcers, suspected malignancy, mucosal lesions. **3. ASPIRATION BIOPSY**: **Fine needle aspiration (FNA)** - aspirate cells/fluid with needle. **Cytology** (not histology). **Indications**: (a) **Cystic swellings** - differentiate cyst vs solid tumor, (b) **Salivary gland lesions**, (c) **Lymph nodes** (metastasis?). **Advantage**: **Minimally invasive**, quick, OPD procedure. **Limitation**: **Cytology** only (no tissue architecture) - less definitive than histology. **4. CORE NEEDLE BIOPSY**: **Larger bore needle** - obtain tissue core (histology possible). **Indications**: Deep tumors (salivary gland), bone lesions. **PRINCIPLES**: **1. Representative sample** - include normal + abnormal tissue (incisional), adequate size. **2. Avoid necrotic areas** - sample ...

questions.question: Describe the procedure for Frenum causing gingival recession.
questions.answer:
Whenever there is an inadequate vestibular depth present, to increase the
retention and stability of the denture, deepening of the vestibule is considered.
Thus vestibuloplasty is a procedure to increase the depth by uncovering the
existing basal bone of the jaws surgically and by repositioning the overlying
mucasa, muscle attachments to a Lower position in the mandible/superior
position in the maxilla.
Vestibuloplasty can be done in the maxilla or in the mandible or both jaws.
Maxillary vestibuloplasty are primarily done on labial/buccalside
while mandibular techniques are done via labial or lingual approach.

questions.question: Write a short note on OPEN SUBMUCOSAL VESTIBULOPLASTY.
questions.answer:
Wallenius advocated an open method to overcome the relapse of clased
vestibuloplasty.
A harizontal incision is made in the mucogingival junction.
A thin mucasal flap is elevated by submucasal dissection followed
by supraperiosteal dissection to the desired extent, excising muscle
and subcutaneous tissues.
Stay sutures are used to fix the flap to the periosteum deep in vestibule.

questions.question: Describe the technique for OPEN SUBMUCOSAL VESTIBULOPLASTY.
questions.answer:
Mucasal flap from inner aspect of lower lip is used to increase the
vestibular depth in anterior mandibular labial vestibule.
The mentalis muscle is detached from the periosteum to required
depth and the vestibule is deepened via supraperiesteal dissection.
The flap is then redirected & placed directly against the periasteum
and sutured at the new depth of the vestibule.
Raw area is left on the Lip side to be healed by secondary intention.
Periosteum of bone is left intact.
Drawback - scarring of mucosa with subsequent decreased flexibility
of lower lip.
ЭЦОГИНОНТ 29 ГИЦАЯТ

questions.question: What is alveoloplasty? Describe indications and procedure.
questions.answer: **Alveoloplasty** is **surgical reshaping/recontouring** of alveolar ridge to create **optimal foundation** for prosthesis (denture). **INDICATIONS**: **1. Irregular/sharp bony ridge** - causes denture discomfort, sore spots. **2. Undercuts** - prevent denture insertion/seating. **3. Post-extraction** (multiple teeth) - smooth sharp interdental/interradicular septa. **4. Protruding/flared labial plate** - improper denture support. **5. Prominent/sharp mylohyoid ridge** - interferes with denture flange. **6. Genial tubercles enlargement** - mandibular midline prominence. **7. Maxillary tuberosity enlargement** - reduces interarch space. **TYPES**: **1. Simple/Intraseptal**: Removes **interdental/interradicular bone** only. Done **during/immediately after** multiple extractions. **No flap** raised. **2. Radical/Interseptal**: **Extensive reshaping** with **mucoperiosteal flap**. **Removes** labial/lingual cortical plates, sharp ridges. Done **separate procedure** or with extractions. **PROCEDURE (Simple - at time of extraction)**: (1) **Extract teeth**, (2) **Rongeur/bone file** - remove **sharp bone** (interdental septa), smooth labial/lingual plates, (3) **Digital compression** - compress alveolar bone plates together (reduces ridge width), (4) **Irrigation**, (5) **Primary closure** if possible (approximate socket margins with sutures). **PROCEDURE (Radical - separate surgery)**: (1) **Incision**: **Crestal incision** along ridge ± **vertical releasing incisions**, (2) **Flap elevation**: Full-thickness mucoperiosteal flap (buccal and lingual/palatal), expose bone, (3) **Bone recontouring**: (a) **Rongeur** - remove irregular bone, sharp projections, (b) **Bone file** - smooth surface, (c) **Surgical bur** (with irrigation) - remove large undercuts, excessive bone, (d) Create **gradual, rounded contour**, (4) **Trim excess soft tissue**: Fibrous/redundant tissue excision, (5) **Flap repositioning**: Approximate flaps (tension-free), (6) **Sutures**: Interrupted 3-0...

questions.question: What is frenectomy? Describe types and indications.
questions.answer: **Frenectomy** is **surgical removal** of **frenum** (fold of mucous membrane attaching lip/tongue to underlying structures). **TYPES**: **1. LABIAL FRENECTOMY**: **Maxillary labial** (most common) - between **central incisors** (midline). **Indications**: (a) **Midline diastema** - frenum attachment on incisive papilla prevents closure, (b) **Denture instability** - thick, prominent frenum dislodges denture during lip movement, (c) **Periodontal problem** - frenum pull causes recession. **Procedure**: (a) **Anesthesia** - infiltration, (b) **Hemostat** placed along frenum (crush tissue), (c) **Incision** along both sides of hemostat (diamond/rhomboid shape), (d) **Excise** frenum (including fibrous tissue extending to periosteum), (e) **Blunt dissection** to bone if needed, (f) **Suture** - interrupted (approximate margins). **2. LINGUAL FRENECTOMY** (Frenuloplasty): **Indication**: **Ankyloglossia (tongue-tie)** - restricted tongue movement. **Features**: Inability to protrude tongue beyond incisors, V-shaped tongue tip, speech problems ("l", "r" sounds), difficulty breastfeeding (infants). **Procedure**: (a) **Elevate tongue** - retract upward/forward, (b) **Incision** - midline horizontal incision at frenum base, (c) **Release** attachment to floor of mouth, (d) **Suture** - reposition mucosa (prevent reattachment). **Alternative**: **Z-plasty** (more complex) - prevents scarring/reattachment. **3. BUCCAL FRENECTOMY**: Less common. **Indication**: Frenum pull causes denture instability or gingival recession. **TIMING**: **Labial** - after **orthodontic closure** of diastema (prevents recurrence) OR before denture fabrication. **Lingual** - infancy (if severe feeding problems) or childhood (before speech development). **POST-OPERATIVE**: Analgesics, soft diet, chlorhexidine rinses, suture removal 7 days.

questions.question: What is vestibuloplasty? When is it indicated?
questions.answer: **Vestibuloplasty** is **surgical deepening** of **vestibular sulcus** (space between alveolar ridge and lip/cheek) to **increase denture-bearing area** and improve **denture retention/stability**. **INDICATIONS**: **1. Resorbed alveolar ridge** - severe resorption (long-standing edentulous patient) causes **shallow vestibule**, (2) **Inadequate denture retention** - insufficient sulcus depth for peripheral seal, (3) **High muscle attachments** - muscles attached close to ridge crest (reduce denture stability), (4) **Before implant placement** - create adequate soft tissue coverage. **PRINCIPLES**: **Reposition muscles** (mentalis, buccinator, mylohyoid) **away from ridge crest** (apically/laterally). **Cover denuded bone** with **oral mucosa** or allow **epithelialization**. **TECHNIQUES**: **1. Submucosal vestibuloplasty**: **Incision** at depth of vestibule, **dissect** muscle attachments from bone, **reposition** apically, **secure** with sutures. **Surgical stent** maintains depth during healing. **2. Secondary epithelialization**: **Remove** mucosa from ridge area, expose bone, allow **spontaneous epithelialization** (6-8 weeks). **Stent** essential. **3. Skin/mucosal grafts**: **Split-thickness skin graft** (from thigh) or **palatal graft** to cover denuded area. Better stability. **4. Clark's vestibuloplasty** (mandible): Full-thickness mucoperiosteal flap reflected to depth, muscles repositioned, **stent** holds position. **POST-OPERATIVE**: **Surgical stent** worn **continuous 7-14 days** (holds soft tissue position, prevents relapse). **Analgesics**, soft diet. **Denture fabrication** after **6-8 weeks** (tissue maturation). **COMPLICATIONS**: **Loss of depth** (relapse - most common), **Nerve injury** (mental nerve), **Hemorrhage**, **Infection**, **Graft failure**. **ALTERNATIVE**: **Ridge augmentation** (bone grafting) - preferred in many cases (restores bone height rather than just sulcus depth).

questions.question: What is maxillary tuberosity reduction? When is it needed?
questions.answer: **Maxillary tuberosity reduction** is **surgical removal** of **excess bone/soft tissue** from **maxillary tuberosity** (posterior maxilla - distal to last molar). **INDICATIONS**: **1. Enlarged tuberosity**: Excessive vertical height. **2. Insufficient interarch space** - between maxillary tuberosity and mandibular ridge (prevents proper denture fabrication). **Minimum required**: **12-15 mm** for complete dentures. **3. Fibrous hyperplastic tissue** - redundant soft tissue. **4. Unilateral enlargement** - causes uneven occlusal plane. **TYPES OF ENLARGEMENT**: **1. Soft tissue hyperplasia** - fibrous tissue only (easier to remove). **2. Bony enlargement** - excess bone (requires bone removal). **3. Combination** - both bone and soft tissue. **PROCEDURE**: **1. Assessment**: **Radiograph** (PA/OPG) - determine if **bone** or **soft tissue** enlargement. **Measure interarch space** (calipers/models). **2. Anesthesia**: **PSA block** + **Greater palatine nerve block**. **3. Incision**: **Elliptical/wedge incision** on **crest** of tuberosity (remove wedge of soft tissue ± bone). OR **Circumferential incision** around tuberosity. **4. Tissue removal**: **a) Soft tissue only**: Excise with scalpel. **b) Bone involvement**: (i) **Elevate mucoperiosteal flap**, (ii) **Rongeur/bone file** - remove excess bone, (iii) **Smooth** sharp edges, (iv) **Replace flap**. **5. Hemostasis**: Pressure, electrocautery if needed. **6. Sutures**: Interrupted 3-0 silk (approximate margins). **7. Immediate denture/surgical stent**: Apply pressure, protect, guide healing. **PRECAUTIONS**: **1. Preserve blood supply**: Maintain **palatal soft tissue** (blood supply from greater palatine artery). **Avoid excessive removal** - risk necrosis. **2. Avoid maxillary sinus**: **Check radiograph** - sinus may extend into tuberosity. **Perforation** - close primarily, antibiotics. **3. Limit bone removal**: Excessive reduction causes **ridge resorption**, poor denture support. **COMPLICATIONS**: **H...

questions.question: What is sialolithiasis? Describe clinical features and management.
questions.answer: **Sialolithiasis** - **salivary calculus (stone)** in ductal system of salivary gland. **Most common salivary gland disorder**. **LOCATION**: **Submandibular gland/duct** (80-90%) - **Wharton's duct**. **Reasons**: (1) **Upward flow** (against gravity), (2) **Thick alkaline** secretion, (3) **Higher calcium/phosphate** content, (4) **Long tortuous** duct (12 cm). **Parotid** (5-20%), **Sublingual** (rare). **CLINICAL FEATURES**: **1. Intermittent painful swelling** - **pathognomonic**. **Swelling** of gland **during meals** (saliva production), **subsides** after meal (1-2 hrs). **"Mealtime syndrome"**. **2. Palpable stone** - **bimanual palpation** (submandibular duct - floor of mouth). **Hard, immobile** mass along duct. **3. Reduced salivary flow** - thick/absent saliva from duct orifice (milk duct). **4. Tenderness** - over gland/duct. **5. Secondary infection** - if complete obstruction → **acute sialadenitis** (fever, swelling, pus from duct). **DIAGNOSIS**: **1. Clinical** - mealtime swelling + palpable stone. **2. Radiographs**: **Occlusal radiograph** (80% stones radiopaque - calcium). **Sialography** (contrast in duct - shows stone as **filling defect**) - **AVOID if acute infection** (spread infection). **3. Ultrasound** - detects radiolucent stones, ductal dilation. **4. Sialendoscopy** - endoscopic visualization + removal. **MANAGEMENT**: **MEDICAL** (Small stones <3mm, distal duct): **1. Conservative**: (a) **Hydration** - increase saliva flow, (b) **Sialogogues** - lemon juice, sour candies (stimulate saliva), (c) **Warm compresses** + **Massage** - milk stone distally, (d) **NSAIDs** - pain/inflammation, (e) **Antibiotics** (if infection) - **Amoxicillin-Clavulanate** (covers *S. aureus, Streptococcus*). **2. Stone milking** - **bimanual massage** (push stone anteriorly toward orifice, express). **SURGICAL**: **1. Intraoral removal** (Distal duct stones - palpable in floor of mouth): (a) **Anesthesia** (local), (b) **Dilate duct** orifice, (c) **Inci...

questions.question: What is a ranula? Describe types and management.
questions.answer: **Ranula** - **mucous extravasation pseudocyst** arising from **sublingual gland** (floor of mouth). **Bluish, translucent swelling** ("frog belly" appearance - Latin *rana* = frog). **TYPES**: **1. SIMPLE (Oral) RANULA**: **Confined** to **floor of mouth** (above mylohyoid muscle). **Clinical features**: (a) **Painless, soft, fluctuant** swelling in **floor of mouth** (one side of frenulum), (b) **Bluish/translucent** color (saliva visible through thin mucosa - pathognomonic), (c) **Displaces tongue** upward/laterally (if large), (d) **No pain** (unless infected). **2. PLUNGING (Cervical) RANULA**: **Extends** through mylohyoid muscle into **neck** (submandibular/submental space). **"Plunges"** below mylohyoid. **Clinical features**: (a) **Neck swelling** (submandibular region), (b) **Soft, fluctuant**, non-tender, (c) **May** have oral component (floor of mouth swelling) OR **only neck** swelling, (d) **Differential**: Dermoid cyst, lymphangioma, cystic hygroma, branchial cyst. **DIAGNOSIS**: **1. Clinical** - characteristic bluish oral swelling (floor of mouth). **2. Aspiration** - **thick, ropy, mucoid** fluid (saliva - viscous, egg-white consistency). **Differential from epidermoid** (cheesy material). **3. MRI/CT** - plunging ranula (extent, relation to mylohyoid). **4. NO histopathology** - **pseudocyst** (no epithelial lining - just mucus pool in connective tissue). **MANAGEMENT**: **SIMPLE RANULA**: **1. Observation** - may spontaneously resolve (especially small, recent). **2. Marsupialization**: (a) **Elliptical excision** of cyst roof (create wide opening into mouth), (b) **Suture** cyst lining edges to oral mucosa, (c) **Simple**, outpatient procedure. **Limitation**: **High recurrence** (50%) - gland continues secreting. **3. Excision of sublingual gland**: **Definitive treatment** - **remove entire sublingual gland** (source of saliva). (a) **Intraoral approach**, (b) **Excise sublingual gland** completely (along with ranula), (c) **Low recurrence** (...

questions.question: Describe the temporomandibular joint (TMJ) anatomy and common disorders.
questions.answer: **TMJ ANATOMY**: **Ginglymoarthrodial joint** (hinge + gliding). **Components**: **1. Articular surfaces**: **Mandibular condyle** (ellipsoid) + **Glenoid fossa** of temporal bone + **Articular eminence** (tubercle). **2. Articular disc**: **Fibrocartilage** disc divides joint into **upper** (translation/gliding) and **lower** (rotation/hinge) compartments. **Biconcave** shape. **Attachments**: Anteriorly to **lateral pterygoid muscle** (superior head), posteriorly to **retrodiscal tissue** (bilaminar zone - vascular), medially/laterally to **condyle**. **3. Capsule**: Fibrous capsule surrounds joint. **4. Ligaments**: **Lateral (temporomandibular)** - limits posterior condyle movement. **Sphenomandibular, Stylomandibular** - accessory. **MOVEMENTS**: **Opening**: (1) **Rotation** (0-25mm) - condyle rotates in lower compartment (hinge), (2) **Translation** (>25mm) - condyle + disc glide forward along articular eminence (upper compartment). **Closing**: Reverse. **COMMON TMJ DISORDERS (TMD)**: **1. INTERNAL DERANGEMENT**: **Disc displacement**. **a) Disc displacement with reduction**: Disc **anteriorly displaced** (mouth closed), **clicks/pops** during opening (disc reduces back onto condyle), **full mouth opening**. **Reciprocal click** (opening + closing). **b) Disc displacement without reduction** (closed lock): Disc **permanently displaced anteriorly**, **cannot reduce**, **limited mouth opening** (<30mm), **deviation** to affected side, **no click** (disc stuck). **2. OSTEOARTHRITIS (Degenerative joint disease)**: **Crepitus** (grating sound), pain, limited movement, **radiographic changes** (flattening, osteophytes, sclerosis). **3. MYOFASCIAL PAIN DYSFUNCTION** (MPD): **Muscle pain** (masticatory muscles - masseter, temporalis, pterygoids), **tenderness** on palpation, **limited opening** (muscle spasm), **no joint sounds**. **Etiology**: Stress, bruxism, parafunctional habits. **4. ANKYLOSIS**: **Fusion** of joint - fibrous or bony. **No movement**, severely ...

questions.question: What is the management of temporomandibular disorders (TMD)?
questions.answer: **TMD management** - **conservative first**, surgery **last resort**. **90% improve** with non-surgical therapy. **CONSERVATIVE (First-line)**: **1. Patient education**: Explain condition (usually self-limiting), avoid aggravating factors. **2. Behavior modification**: (a) **Soft diet** - avoid hard, chewy foods, (b) **Avoid wide opening** - no yawning, large bites, (c) **Avoid parafunctional habits** - **no gum chewing**, nail biting, pencil biting, (d) **Stress reduction** - counseling, relaxation techniques. **3. Physical therapy**: (a) **Moist heat** - warm compress (20 min, 3-4x/day) - muscle relaxation, (b) **Ice packs** - acute pain (first 24-48 hrs), (c) **Jaw exercises** - gentle stretching (improve mobility, muscle strength), (d) **Massage** - masticatory muscles (masseter, temporalis). **4. Pharmacotherapy**: (a) **NSAIDs** - **Ibuprofen 400mg TDS** (pain, inflammation), (b) **Muscle relaxants** - **Diazepam 5mg** or **Chlorzoxazone** (muscle spasm, especially nighttime bruxism), (c) **Tricyclic antidepressants** - **Amitriptyline 10-25mg** (chronic pain, nocturnal bruxism). **5. Occlusal splint (Bite guard)**: **Stabilization splint** - **hard acrylic**, covers maxillary/mandibular teeth, worn **nighttime**. **Functions**: (a) Protects teeth from bruxism, (b) **Repositions** condyle-disc relationship (controversial), (c) **Muscle relaxation** (reduces hyperactivity), (d) **Cognitive awareness** (reminds patient not to clench). **Indications**: Bruxism, muscle pain, mild disc displacement with reduction. **MINIMALLY INVASIVE**: **6. Arthrocentesis**: **Lavage** of upper joint space (wash out inflammatory mediators, lyse adhesions). **Indications**: **Closed lock** (disc displacement without reduction - acute), limited opening, pain. **Procedure**: **2 needles** in superior joint space, irrigate with **Ringer's lactate** (200-300 mL). **Success**: 70-80% (improves opening, reduces pain). **7. Arthroscopy**: **Endoscopic** visualization + treatment (lysis o...

questions.question: What is TMJ dislocation? How do you reduce it?
questions.answer: **TMJ dislocation** - **condyle displaced** anteriorly **beyond articular eminence**, **cannot return** to glenoid fossa. **Bilateral** more common than unilateral. **ETIOLOGY**: **Wide opening** - yawning, laughing, prolonged dental treatment, trauma, seizures. **Predisposing**: **Lax ligaments**, **shallow fossa**, **previous dislocations**. **CLINICAL FEATURES**: **1. Mouth wide open** - **cannot close**. **2. Drooling** - cannot swallow saliva. **3. Speech difficulty**. **4. Bilateral**: **Prognathic** appearance (chin protrudes forward). **Preauricular depressions** (empty fossa palpable). **5. Unilateral**: **Deviation** to non-dislocated side, **open bite** opposite side. **6. Muscles in spasm** - painful, difficult to reduce (time-dependent). **7. Preauricular pain**. **TYPES**: **Acute** (<24-48 hrs), **Chronic/Recurrent** (repeated episodes), **Chronic** (>1 month unreduced). **REDUCTION (Manual - Hippocratic method)**: **1. Position**: Patient **seated** (head supported), operator **in front** or **behind** patient (behind easier). **2. Muscle relaxation**: (a) **Reassure** patient (anxiety worsens spasm), (b) **Local anesthesia** - inject into lateral pterygoid muscle/joint capsule (optional), (c) **Sedation/GA** if severe spasm (chronic dislocation), (d) **Wait** 10-15 min (muscle relaxation/fatigue). **3. Thumb placement**: Operator places **thumbs** on **occlusal surfaces** of lower molars (wrap thumbs with gauze - prevent bite injury). **Fingers** grasp **inferior border** of mandible externally. **4. Downward pressure**: **Press down firmly** on molars (disimpacts condyle from anterior to eminence, moves condyle **inferiorly**). **5. Backward rotation**: While maintaining downward pressure, **push chin backward and upward** (guides condyle **posteriorly** into fossa). **6. Reduction**: **Palpable/audible click** - condyle relocates. **Mouth closes** (patient bites down). **Quickly remove thumbs** (reflex bite). **POST-REDUCTION**: (1) **Soft diet** ...
